reasoning_summary: Run 20260421_192024_bf16_gemm_v1_8fd88cb4 at 24.693696 ms restored the compact 128x128 PTX anchor almost exactly: it is only about 0.0045 ms slower than the accepted 24.689153 ms base, with the same 201 registers/thread, 22,016 B shared-memory footprint, 16.61% active warps, and nearly identical tensor activity and stall mix. That means the round-11 256x128 loss is fully isolated and the search can continue on a clean compact PTX surface again. On this restored anchor, the strongest live next move is to collapse the per-tile wait/sync seam into a pairwise stage advance, because the current loop still executes a wait_group_0 plus __syncthreads handoff on every next_tile boundary while barrier is 8.20% and long-scoreboard is 5.17%. I rank a narrower cadence trim second as the lower-disruption alternative on the same 22,016 B surface. I keep the 2-K-stage Pg2s port third because the latest round reinforced how dangerous larger shared-memory surfaces are on this GPU, so that family should stay behind the compact-surface sync experiments until they are exhausted.
family_audit: accept: the compact 128x128 PTX grouped_rows=4 anchor has been successfully restored and is again the live search surface.
reject_this_round: do not reopen the failed 256x128 writer-safe branch or any other high-shared geometry variant until the compact sync families are exhausted.
keep_open: pairwise wait/sync collapse, bounded cadence trim, and guarded 2-K-stage Pg2s remain the three useful families from the restored compact anchor.

directions.name: Collapse The Compact PTX Wait-Sync Seam Into A Pairwise Stage Advance
directions.hypothesis: The restored compact PTX loop still waits and synchronizes on every next_tile boundary before deciding whether a future refill exists. Recasting that handoff into a pairwise stage advance should reduce barrier and scoreboard pressure together without reopening the high-shared 256x128 loss surface.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier and long-scoreboard are the clearest remaining compact-surface bottlenecks on the restored anchor.
directions.risk: Moderate. This stays on the compact PTX hot loop, but it directly touches the synchronization seam that guards the async-copy pipeline.
ranking_notes: The restored anchor proved the wide-tiling loss is gone, so the next high-signal move is to attack the compact loop's explicit wait/sync seam directly.
This family already has positive history in the search tree, but it has not been sampled from the current restored anchor.
It preserves the winning 22,016 B shared-memory surface and avoids mixing another geometry change into the same round.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the pairwise advance requires extra hot-loop predicates or any third shared-memory stage, or if correctness fails on the first buildable attempt.
expected_local_changes: Refactor the compact PTX hot loop so wait/sync advances in pairwise stages instead of on every single next_tile boundary.
Keep grouped_rows=4, 128x128 CTA geometry, and the 22,016 B shared-memory footprint unchanged.
Do not mix this experiment with a tiling or export-path change.

directions.name: Trim The Compact PTX Wait Group And Sync Cadence Without Growing Shared Memory
directions.hypothesis: If the pairwise collapse proves too invasive, a narrower retime of wait_group and __syncthreads placement on the restored compact surface can still attack barrier without reopening the shared-memory or geometry risks from recent failures.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier handoff overhead is still large enough to justify a smaller cadence-only probe on the compact PTX surface.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is narrower than dir_01, but it still edits fragile synchronization placement in the hot loop.
ranking_notes: This is the lower-disruption fallback if the pairwise collapse looks too invasive in code review.
It keeps the compact PTX surface intact and only adjusts the timing of the existing wait/sync seam.
It ranks second because a true pairwise stage advance has a better chance of reducing both barrier and scoreboard together.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the trim increases long-scoreboard or forces any shared-memory growth beyond the current 22,016 B footprint.
expected_local_changes: Retime wait_group and __syncthreads placement inside the compact PTX loop without changing stage depth.
Keep grouped_rows=4, the 128x128 dispatch, and the shared-memory budget unchanged.
Avoid pairing this with any pointer-hoist or tiling change.

directions.name: Keep The 2-K-Stage Pg2s Port Parked Behind The Compact Sync Experiments
directions.hypothesis: The 2-K-stage Pg2s port may still amortize waits, but after the failed 256x128 reopen it should remain behind the compact sync families unless the implementation can clearly avoid replaying the large-shared-budget failure mode.
directions.expected_bottleneck: If revisited later, the target is still compact-surface latency hiding and copy cadence, not a new geometry surface.
directions.risk: High. This family still threatens shared-memory growth and a worse residency regime unless it is very carefully constrained.
ranking_notes: This family stays in the tree because it still targets the right high-level problem, but it has lower priority after the recent shared-budget loss evidence.
It ranks behind both compact sync experiments because those are cleaner and cheaper on the restored anchor.
Only revisit it if the compact wait/sync families flatten out again.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the port requires a larger shared-memory surface or drops the compact anchor below the current two-CTA register-limited regime.
expected_local_changes: Keep the compact 128x128 PTX dispatch active.
Only port the 2-K-stage preload/drain structure if the shared-memory budget remains tightly controlled.
Avoid combining the port with any tiling or launch-order change.

notes: Round 13/20 diagnosis emitted from the restored compact PTX anchor; frontier should now prefer compact-surface sync experiments over any new geometry branch.
